speaker: interviewer
text: Interview of Henry Dressler, June 29, 1995. Henry, I want to go back to the notification of deportation for your family. What could your family take with you as you were notified that you were going to deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the police picked us up, they did not tell us anything about any deportation procedure. They just told you, we have to come to the police quarters to-- and then they talk to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our, our passports or papers were invalidated when we got to that hall where they all assembled at. And that's when they said, well, if you want to go home to pick up a suitcase, you, you can do that. And, and that was about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after we came to Poland, if I remember right, they asked for-- they said you can ask for permission for one in the family to go back for three days to Germany to liquidate things. And we did not do this, because we did not want to separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive any packages from friends or family in Germany at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, our aunt packed up some of our belongings, even if I remember right, shipped some of our furniture to, to Poland-- which, which we got after a month or a couple of months-- and shipped some clothes. And she more or less liquidated our apartment and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but not the business. What happened to the business-- it was just closed up, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this aunt who sent you things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Jetti, J-E-T-T-I. Her married name was Kohn. Kohn is-- I think it was K-O-H-N, I'm not sure, or C-O-H-N, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she lived in Cologne. It was an older sister of my father's.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband had died a year before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were being driven to the train station--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did people in Dresden react to this, to your being driven to the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we were driven in large transporter, like two police transporter, open ones. We have to sit in 10 rows of five, six people each. And we were driven through the whole city, and the people stood, of course, and, and looking what, what, what was going on. There were-- Dresden was a busy street with a lot of people walking around. It's not like today when you don't see anybody in a, in a, in a city. It was a lively street, and they all saw, saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the reasons that your family was being deported to Poland along with the others that you mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the transport was mostly Polish citizens. There was a discussion between the two governments. I'm not a historian, so I, I-- you can probably tell this better than I. Research it. There was a disagreement with the Polish government. The Polish government had a-- knew they had a lot of Polish citizens in Germany or abroad, and they wanted-- they issued a law that whoever was not in Poland up to a certain date would lose their Polish citizenship. And the Germans were not interested in having any Polish Jews in their country. They wanted to get rid of Jews. They didn't want to get more. And that's why they did this without notifying the Polish government. How we got in, I'm not quite sure. I don't think we were Polish citizen. I think we were stateless most of the time. There was a state of-- there's a nationality, being stateless. We were not German citizens. We never had-- my father never had a German passport. We were stateless. Somehow-- but he came from the part where other Polish Jews would come from, from Austria. And I'm not quite sure what happened-- that's how we got on-- in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now let's go back to your entrance into Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: May I say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: An incident that I want to point out to me. In, in Germany, every year, the Nazi party had a, a party rally-- I think it was either in Munich or in Nuremberg-- where probably hundred thousands of Germans in, in their brown uniform, the brown shirts, assembled, and where they had hundreds of, of flags and the swastikas and the banners. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, there was no television, but we saw it on radio, and we, we saw it all over. And, and Hitler would come and stand on top of these hundred thousands of people, and he would speak to them. And, and when he spoke, he, he was a very strong speaker. He almost hypnotized the people. And he, and he, he, he excited them. And, and, and he, he instilled them with hatred, and, and with venom. And, and to me, that was one of the most scary things to, to, to witness. And I always remember these big assemblies there, where, where the people stood, and they were screaming sieg hail and, and, and adoring this man. And up to this day, when I hear any, any speaker who speaks with, with so much, with so much enthusiasm-- maybe that's the wrong word-- and so strongly with so much emotion, I get scared. Because if this is used in the wrong way, I think it's a very dangerous thing. As a matter of fact, I saw when I was in St. Louis in a museum, a picture of one of these rallies they had there, and I was quite, quite touched by this picture, seeing this. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right. Now let's go back to your entrance into Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you and your family were in Poland, how did you end up in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we were picked up in, in, at the border, they said, where do you want to go? And we were transported, about 1,400 people, but I think at that day, all the-- on the Polish border there must have been, I think, about 40,000 people who were deported from Germany into Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked us, where do you want to go? And well, we really had nobody where to go, but my father served in the Austrian army in the first World War. And my father had a very good memory for people he ever met. And he tried-- keep in touch with them. And he knew that his mother's family came from Cracow. And it was a nearer city to go, really, so we said to go to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put us on a train to Cracow, and there we were on the railroad station all of a sudden standing there with basically no place to go. And then we were very fortunate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somebody called a family who were very common in Cracow and very, very wealthy and asked them if they had-- they have four people here, if they need quarter-- if they, if they would take them in. And their name was Wasserberger, name of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell that name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, W-A-S-S-E-R-B-E-R-G-E-R, Wasserberger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what businesses were they involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had flour mills, and they had, if I remember right, a monopoly for rice for the import into China. They were very, very-- it's big business people, very nice family. It was started by an old man, we call him the old Mr. Wasserberger, and he had three sons and one daughter but owned the business in different areas. And, and that Mrs. Wasserberger said, well, she would take them in if the man who called her would personally guarantee for us, guarantee for us, because obviously they had a very, a very nice home. And she said they did, and, and they said, bring them, bring them over in a taxi. And all of a sudden, we were in that very nice house. And they had a, a gym on the top floor. And they converted this gym into a bedroom for-- into a room for us, which was a couple of beds. And they had a, a shower in the bathroom next to the gym. And that was, like, four stories with, you know, different levels. And the maids cooked for us right away and brought us some food. And that's how we were very, very fortunate, because many others had to go into mass quarters and sleep in the, in the straw somewhere, and who were not, who were not as fortunate. In all, we were very fortunate in all the things that happened. A lot of things happened to us, and we always appreciated this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and your father and the other members of your family do to earn a living during this time you were in Cracow living with this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did not do much. I started working. I was looking for some work. And I started working for a small import firm, just like a one-man business somewhere, to do foreign correspondence, to do German correspondence and some English correspondence while he was out of town and sort of look after his, his business. My father didn't really do much. My parents didn't do much, you know. No, I was really the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sister? Did she work in this period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember. I, I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contacts with the Jewish community in Cracow in this period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Oh, yes. We must have had. I don't remember. Well, there were, there were classes organized for us to learn Polish. So we had classes in, in the Polish language there. And of course we-- the refugees met among each other, I am sure. There was-- I would say yes. I cannot pinpoint it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Cracow from '38 until the start of World War I-- or World War II, rather. Did you expect the invasion in September of 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the people who took us in-- my sister was very sick. Somehow got sick. She had something. And the man, the, the senior of that family, he sent my sister to a, to a, to a spa somewhere near, near Cracow, called Rabka-- no, not Rabka. Zakopane-- for a few, for a few weeks. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and one day he came to us. We know politically, situation wasn't good. He came into us, into our house and said, I just called your daughter. I told her to come back home right away. We knew there was, there was-- things were not good. And we knew they were drafting all the, the, the soldiers. And I didn't sleep home for a few nights. I didn't want to be, I didn't want to be pulled into the army. We knew something was going to happen. And on September 1, 1938, all of a sudden the morning, the bombing of Cracow started. And we didn't know what to do. We took, we took a handkerchief, put water on it or something, and hold in front of our nose, in case there was a gas-- gas bombs. You don't know. And the, the Germans, Germans invaded Cracow and didn't have any-- the Polish army didn't have a chance. After three days, the Germans occupied Cracow. There was some bombing and some damage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the Jewish-- what did the Jewish family do who had taken you in as the Germans advanced?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they were well-connected. They ran away. They fled. They had, they had good cars. And they had, had chauffeurs. And they went-- they fled east.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a funny thing, they came back in August '38, and I think there was a World's Fair in America. I think that's what it was. And we asked them, how's the situation? They'll say, isn't going to be a war. So they, they didn't know. As, as close as they worked with the government, they did not know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they, they all fled. The whole family fled. And we didn't know where they were. We found out later they all made it and wound up in, in England and Argentina and, and in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the house that you were living in after the Germans occupied Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, the maids, the maids stayed in the house. They had a maid on every floor. They stayed in the house. The German army occupied it. And since it was one of the better houses in Cracow, after two-- first of all, there were 70 German soldiers, and we had our little room up there in the, in the basement. It was, it was actually a door-- with a store-- a closed-up store and was an entrance to the back. And we were hiding in there. And when first, when they first came in, they searched the house. And the German army captain or a lieutenant, whatever it was, came in, and he saw us there. And, and the first thing he said, turn on the radio. He wanted to know what station we were listening to, because it was a favorite thing to listen to a Russian station. And said well, we listen to German stations because we speak only German. And it was really on, on a German station. And he said, all right. He, he let us stay there. And then the commandant of the, the-- of Cracow-- the army commandant of Cracow, the, the highest army officer, moved into this house. He took over one floor. And some high-- the quartermaster, some high officers of the civilian army, not the SS army, the other army, moved in there, and we-- they let us stay there. We kept a very, very low profile. We, we, we got out of the window so we didn't have to go through the, through the, through the hall, and so we managed. We stayed there.  And at that time, we were protected a little bit by, by the fact there was always a German guard outside. So the-- like the Germans who made raids into, into, into houses where Jewish people lived, did not come in there. So we-- again, it was one of the things that we were lucky for awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say was the biggest change in life in Cracow after the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a occupation by a hostile force. First of all, there was a curfew at night. We had to get up at 1 o'clock at night to stand in line for bread in the morning, until somebody in the morning-- if you got something, you were lucky. And it was, it was winter. And the winters in Poland got very cold. And of course, the Jews, as of December 1, '39, we had to wear a Levite armband with a, with a Star of David. And Jews were picked up from the street by German army and SS groups who threw them onto the truck if they need workers. They picked, just picked them up from the street and had to put them on their truck and had to take them to work. And nobody knew where they were. And most of them came back. Some of them, they never came back. Some of them were beaten up. That was a steady thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this ever happen to you or any member of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Well, when the Germans-- soon after the Germans took over, I got a job in the Jewish community or the Jewish council or Jewish community center or Jewish federation, whatever you want to call, because they needed German-speaking people who could handle German correspondence and German shorthand. And that was up my alley. And somebody knew about me, and I got a job and I was a-- I handled all the typing there. And at one point, they, they put a typing office where I was in charge and had six, eight girls working there. And I distribute the work for typing. Because it was like a-- like a small town, like a community. It had, it had offices for, for, for housing, offices for work, different, different sections. And so that's, that's where I worked. The pay was probably very minimal. I don't think my father did anything. My sister may have done some sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still living in the first house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the attitude of the Poles towards the treatment of Polish Jews-- of the Jews in this period in Cracow, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were always a few very good Jews, very good Poles, who were friendly to them. I-- I cannot really answer that question.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have much contact with the Polish community, the Polish Christian community?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some kind. I know I was hit over the head once walking in a, in a park for being Jew, and that was before Hitler came in and before the Germans came in. And I'd rather not go into this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At this period of time after the Germans occupied Cracow, did your parents, your family, ever consider going into hiding or trying to obtain false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish people had a-- had roots there, had a, had a support base. We were in a foreign country. We did not speak the language. And for four people to get false papers or go into hiding-- and if they had to go into hiding, they had to be hidden by Polish people. And we did not have the connection. We did not think about it. And it would've been a foolish thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other German Jews were there like you in Cracow in this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. No, I mean, there must've been some. And I don't know. I know one of my-- two of my cousins from Dresden came there, too. One died in, in Plaszow, and the other one later went somewhere, somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of these two cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Karl, K-A-R-L. He died-- actually, my father's cousin-- he, he died in Plaszow very miserably. The other one was Max. And he, he lived with us for a little while when we stayed with the Wasserbergers, and then he was sent out, too. And then he survived somewhere. And he was married to a German, non-Jewish woman and-- who divorced him. And he was Jewish. And after the war, he went back to her to Germany, and they got remarried. And then he died after a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you told that you were going to be moved into the Cracow ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the month that you moved into the ghetto in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was March '41. March '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you notified about this move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know, you just-- you just know. [LAUGHS] I think we had-- we all had to have IDs already. And it came an order out that if you-- you had to have some IDs, permission to stay in Cracow. You could get this only if you worked or if you-- I think for elderly. And they gave you-- they gave an order to leave everybody else, but they left it open to you where you wanted to go. And you had to apply for permission to take the train, let's say, to Warsaw, or to, to, to Lvov, to [NON-ENGLISH] or so. I think that's what happened. And then we knew the, the ghetto in Cracow Podgorze was-- all the Poles who lived in there were-- had to move out. And they built the walls around-- high walls around the ghetto. We knew. And I worked in the Jewish office there. So we knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the day that you and your family moved into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, we packed our little bundles. We probably had a little pushcart or something. And we, we, we went in there. And we found a place to live there. My father knew, from the first World War, a doctor, Dr. Blassberg. And they, they were very small, small apartments, very small rooms. And there wasn't really no room for-- no apartments. He had an apartment. And he was allowed, as a doctor, an extra room as a waiting room for patients.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he slept in his office. I had a waiting room. And my parents had what would have been the, the living room, I guess, and with, with two beds in it. There was one bathroom and one kitchen. And that's, that's how we lived. How would you describe food supplies at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Food became tight. But at that point, most people who lived in who had, still had resources in Cracow managed to have some, some resources. Some food was smuggled in at the ghetto. Poles still would come in or the-- or work groups went outside of the ghetto, and it was smuggled in. It was all dangerous. But food at that time was-- was still available. But then later on, it got very, very tight.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you avoid any of the roundups in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, personally? Well, you got to be lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any particular time when you thought you were going to get caught in a roundup in the ghetto? In the Cracow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was one point we were very, very close to-- there were always the roundup-- I guess what you call roundups, where they-- we called it [GERMAN], resettlements or Aktions where they take 5,000 people and they ship them out. And there was one in, I think it was '43 or '44, probably in '44. You saw in the picture, in the movie, where they threw the luggage out of the-- from down from the-- from the catwalk from the, from the deck. They went door by door, and we and the whole family were pushed down. We were in the Plac Zgody, the one square in, in the ghetto where they assembled people. And when the place was full, they pulled up the trucks and they took them to the trains to send out east.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents, my sister, and I were down there and just so happened that one German saw somebody who knew me from my work came and said to me, Dressler, [GERMAN], what are you doing here? It's a stupid question, of course. And he said, where is your family? And I said, here. He said, come with me. And he called another German over. And he moved us over to a small square, maybe the size of this room and he said, you stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, why he did it, I don't know. I was not friendly with him, or so. He just knew me. He did it. The strange thing in this was, my sister had a girlfriend who stood with us and she-- as he started, she, she had her hand, her arm, on my sister's. And when he said, come with me, she went with us. And at that point, this saved her life. So it shows how, how, how much your life was worth was an instant, a, a little moment. And we stood there on that square that was in front of an orphanage, or of a children's home. And there we saw, while we were waiting-- they kept us there until the whole thing was over, and then we could go back to our homes. While we were waiting, there was a, a children's home. We saw how a truck pulled up, and the Germans went up into this children's home and, and threw the children down on the truck. That was a-- the other angle of it. And then you kept on living for the next thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any other acts of brutality in the Cracow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, I've seen shootings and beatings, plenty beatings. I've seen when we first-- no, that was in Plaszow. There was plenty beating and plenty shooting. There were big Aktion-- whenever there were these Aktions, there was always-- people were shot. And I think that, in the ghetto in Cracow-- if I may talk about Schindler for a moment-- he already employed people in his satellite camp in, in Cracow. But he was friends with [NON-ENGLISH], and he, he would go on horseback one side of the ghetto where, where, where there was a hill, and he would stand on top of the hill. I think they showed this in the movie, where he looked down and saw what was going on. I think it was that point that Hitler began to see that it was-- that the Jews were not only a money-making proposition, but that he had to-- he would try to save them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anyone at this point who was working in Schindler's factory while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I knew some. See in the first, they all lived in the ghetto, and they moved. They came out in the morning and came back at night. Only later on, he established his own little camp where he kept them overnight. And one day-- you may have seen this in the picture-- he didn't let them go home at night, because he knew there was something going on. There was plenty going on in, in, in the ghetto. It was, it was a bad time. And of course, things got scarce. And, and medical facilities, we had a, a hospital in there. And one, I think one of the last, last Aktions, they cleaned out the whole hospital of all patients and some of the doctors and just shot them. And these are some of the things that went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, during this period of time, you were still working for the Jewish Council in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In between-- one day a week or so, I worked outside somewhere. I had to work outside somewhere. And this was almost like a holiday. Because you can smell some fresh air, you-- at the airfield, at the army airfield, you, you had to dig, dig up the field. And you went there by truck outside of the camp. It, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to keep up your spirits during the time you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, [LAUGHS] you either have your spirits, or you don't. You hope. You keep-- you just keep hope. You keep, you keep living. And you, you try to have a normal life as much as-- I remember in the ghetto, there were young, young, a few young people. I was a teenager at that time, and boys and girls. There was a 9 o'clock curfew. We got together after work, and we sat in a little room, and we sang songs. Or if somebody had a record player, we'd play some, we'd play some records. And you do-- you do a little socializing within the limitations that the ghetto permitted. And that's how we kept up our, our spirits.
